deepseek-r1-distill-llama-8b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this patient.
Let me go through the information step by step.
The patient is a 34-year-old woman who fell on her outstretched right hand, causing moderate right wrist pain.
She also reports numbness in the 3 medial digits.
She has no prior medical conditions, no medications, and no relevant family history.
Her vital signs are within normal range except for a slightly elevated blood pressure, but that's probably due to the pain.
On physical exam, she can flex only the lateral two digits when making a fist.
That suggests that the ulnar nerve might be involved because the ulnar nerve controls the small muscles of the hand, specifically the intrinsic muscles.
When she taps the anterior wrist, the medial three digits tingle, which aligns with the area where the ulnar nerve innervates.
In the context of a fall on an outstretched hand, the most common injury is a distal radial fracture, but the physical findings here point more towards a nerve injury.
The ulnar nerve is commonly injured at the wrist, often due to a fall or repetitive use.
The inability to flex the lateral two digits and the tingling in the medial three digits are classic signs of an ulnar nerve injury, specifically a ulnar nerve lesion at the wrist.
So, putting it all together, the most likely diagnosis is a ulnar nerve lesion, also known as a cubital tunnel syndrome or a Guyon's canal stenosis.
But since the presentation is acute after a fall, it's more likely an injury rather than a chronic condition.
The patient's symptoms and physical exam findings are indicative of an ulnar nerve injury.
The inability to flex the lateral two digits and the tingling in the medial three digits, along with the mechanism of injury, point to a ulnar nerve lesion.
